text: Hello, I'm sharing a RTAP inguinal hernia in a patient with a large indirect right inguinal hernia. The only thing a little bit different for me in this case is that the patient has had a robotic prostatectomy before, so tissue planes are a little bit stickier than normal because of the scarring of the previous dissection. But I'm starting the case as I normally do, going medially down towards the pubic tubercle, Here you can see some scarring anterior to the bladder. Most of the time in these prostatectomy cases after the bladder flap is dropped down, it is usually not tacked back up.

The surgeon dissects the hernia sac emerging from the indirect space, encountering sticky tissue during medial dissection towards the pubic bone. The surgeon carefully works laterally to locate the cord structures, avoiding injury. Several peritoneal holes are created during sac reduction, which will be patched using the hernia sac itself.

A small lipoma is visible, displaced during the dissection, and set aside. The surgeon continues dissecting the hernia sac, revealing a hole in the tissue. The edge of the sac is identified, and the chord structures are swept down and away from the surgical field.

The surgeon continues to parietalize the cord structures, working in a challenging space near the femoral and obturator regions. The tissue planes are heavily scarred, complicating the dissection. The pubic tubercle is identified, and the surgeon attempts to parietalize the vas deferens. A hole is created in the peritoneum, and the surrounding fat obscures the femoral vessels, necessitating further dissection to ensure proper mesh placement.

The surgeon revisits the lipoma, amputating it to facilitate further parietalization of the cord structures. The dissection continues over the femoral area, revealing a hemo lock clip amidst scar tissue from a previous lymph node dissection. The removal of the clip provides space for the mesh to lay flat, reducing the risk of hernia recurrence.

The surgeon carefully places a twelve by sixteen mesh into the pocket created during dissection, serially unfolding it to ensure proper fit. The mesh is positioned satisfactorily, and the flap is closed to secure the repair.

I certainly could have chosen to do this case in an open fashion and avoid this part of the dissection. I don't think there's anything wrong with that. In the end, however, I feel like this was a very good repair.

Additionally, I feel that the preservation of the hernia sac in this case was beneficial in helping me close the rents that were inadvertently created. In a case like this, had I abandoned the sack, I wouldn't have had that extra tissue to help me and I would have probably had to just close them primarily, which is not the end of the world. But here I'm just working with this loose redundant tissue to create a little flap patch over top of the defects. Lots of ways to do this. This just seemed like the easiest and most natural to me. It always amazes me how redundant the tissue is and ability to close even what appeared to be large gaps. And that concludes the procedure. I hope you enjoyed.
